interactions.summary: Jiang explains the church's sacramental and judicial powers: priests control interpretation, sacraments channel divine force, the church assigns purgatory and sainthood, and heresy becomes refusal of church authority after instruction.
He catalogs church corruption as legal practice: indulgences, simony, relic commerce, worldly clerics, tithes, and landholding transform spiritual authority into wealth and privilege.

Jiang pivots from corruption to legitimacy: a church's authority depends on enforcing orthodoxy, and around 1000 it faces five pressures beginning with Muslim control of Jerusalem and the comparative success of Muslim Spain.

claims.claim: Priestly control over preaching and the Bible gives clerics power by making authorized interpretation a church monopoly.
claims.temporal_scope: Medieval church model in this lecture.

claims.claim: The Eucharist is presented as a ritual where priests channel Jesus into bread and wine, a practice Jiang says later Protestants will reject as superstitious.
claims.temporal_scope: Medieval practice and later Protestant reaction as explained on 2025-03-20.

claims.claim: Heresy is defined not as initial misunderstanding but as refusal to admit church authority after the church has educated someone.
Jiang argues that indulgences, simony, relic sales, worldly clerics, tithes, and landholding turned the church's spiritual authority into corruption and special privilege.
A church's legitimacy depends on enforcing orthodoxy; as disagreement spreads, the church's authority decreases.
claims.temporal_scope: General institutional model in this lecture.

claims.claim: Muslim control of Jerusalem and Muslim Spain's wealth, cosmopolitanism, innovation, and tolerance are presented as challenges to Catholic legitimacy.
claims.temporal_scope: Around the year 1000 as framed in this lecture.

signature_moments.moment: The priest does not merely symbolize Jesus; in Jiang's description, the rite makes people eat and drink Jesus.
signature_moments.source_phrase: You eat Jesus, you drink Jesus
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It preserves the strangeness that later Protestant rejection will target.

signature_moments.moment: Excommunication is not social exclusion only; it is removal from the community that controls hell.
signature_moments.source_phrase: you will burn in hell forever
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The phrase shows why institutional discipline has cosmic force.

signature_moments.moment: Orthodoxy is power: the church must enforce right thinking or its authority dissolves.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the church must enforce right thinking
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It names the mechanism that drives the rest of the lecture's persecution and crusade logic.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: A payment to the church that Jiang describes as reducing time in purgatory; he calls it spiritual bribery.
Buying one's way into church office regardless of education or theological understanding.
Right or correct thinking enforced by the church as the basis of institutional legitimacy.

chronology_notes.note: The lecture places these legitimacy problems around the year 1000, before the First Crusade.
